rubric: See ground_truth/RUBRIC.md — label for DURATION budgeting, not literary theory.

labels.visual_event: A hereditary estate set well back from the road: hedges, locking gates, broken greenhouses, a large shady garden of box-bordered paths and grape-covered arbors. The house stands empty-looking, handsome, and wrong.

next_beat.dialogue: It is very seldom that mere ordinary people like John and myself secure ancestra…

labels.visual_event: A big airy room at the top of the house, nearly the whole floor. Windows look all ways-barred. Rings and things in the walls. A great heavy bedstead. The wallpaper is stripped in great patches: a sprawling flamboyant pattern, smouldering unclean yellow, dull yet lurid orange in places, sickly sulphur in others. THE NARRATOR, late twenties, pale, slight, hair simply dressed, in a light summer gown, stands taking it in. She carries a small hidden journal and pencil. JOHN, thirties, upright, practical, physician's neat summer suit, watches her with fond authority.

next_beat.dialogue: Still I will proudly declare that there is something queer about it.

labels.visual_event: He smiles, already certain.

prev_beat.dialogue: We took the nursery on your account. Perfect rest.

next_beat.dialogue: John is practical in the extreme.

labels.visual_event: Later. THE NARRATOR sits by a window with her journal, writing in quick secret strokes. Sunshine pours in. Below: garden, arbors, a shaded lane toward a private wharf and the bay.

next_beat.dialogue: If a physician of high standing, and one's own husband, assures friends and rela…

labels.visual_event: He checks the barred windows, satisfied, and goes. She exhales, exhausted by the performance of calm.
Moonlight. THE NARRATOR lies on the nailed-down bed. John sleeps beside her. She stares at the wallpaper: lame uncertain curves that plunge off at outrageous angles.

next_beat.dialogue: I don't like our room a bit.

labels.visual_event: She rises softly, touches the wall. A draught. John stirs.

prev_beat.dialogue: The color is repellant, almost revolting; a smouldering, unclean yellow, strange…

next_beat.dialogue: What is it, little girl? Don't go walking about like that-you'll get cold.

labels.visual_event: Box-bordered paths, long grape arbors with seats, riotous old-fashioned flowers, gnarly trees. THE NARRATOR walks slowly, supervised by distance. JENNIE-John's sister, capable, neat, enthusiastic housekeeper-cuts roses, watches her with bright care.

next_beat.dialogue: There is a delicious garden! I never saw such a garden.

labels.visual_event: Two weeks in. THE NARRATOR at the window again, journal open. She is thinner in the face; the effort of dressing shows.

next_beat.dialogue: We have been here two weeks, and I haven't felt like writing before, since that …

labels.visual_event: She looks toward a closed door-somewhere below, a baby cries once, then is soothed.

prev_beat.dialogue: He knows there is no reason to suffer, and that satisfies him.

next_beat.dialogue: It is fortunate Mary is so good with the baby. Such a dear baby!

labels.visual_event: He takes her in his arms, fond, dismissing.

prev_beat.dialogue: Then do let us go downstairs. There are such pretty rooms there.

next_beat.dialogue: Blessed little goose. I would go down cellar if you wished, and have it whitewas…

labels.visual_event: THE NARRATOR alone, tracing the pattern with her eyes from the bed. A recurrent spot: the pattern lolls like a broken neck; two bulbous eyes stare upside-down. Where two breadths failed to match, the eyes go up and down the line.

next_beat.dialogue: I'm really getting quite fond of the big room, all but that horrid paper.

labels.visual_event: JENNIE's figure appears far down the winding road. THE NARRATOR hides the journal at once. JENNIE enters with clean linen, cheerful, thorough.

prev_beat.dialogue: I never saw so much expression in an inanimate thing before.

next_beat.dialogue: Writing again? Rest is what you need. I verily believe it is the writing which m…

labels.visual_event: JENNIE smiles, unconvinced, and begins to tidy. She smooths a yellow smooch on the baseboard without comment-yet.

prev_beat.dialogue: I was only looking at the garden.

labels.visual_event: JOHN sits beside her, professional kindness.

next_beat.dialogue: If you don't pick up faster I shall send you to Weir Mitchell in the fall.

labels.visual_event: He doses her with cod-liver oil, tonics; pours a little wine. Rare meat waits on a tray.

prev_beat.dialogue: Don't let any silly fancies run away with you.

next_beat.dialogue: Dear John! He loves me very dearly, and hates to have me sick.

labels.visual_event: She tries to speak earnestly. Tears come before she finishes.

next_beat.dialogue: I wish you would let me go and make a visit to Cousin Henry and Julia.

labels.visual_event: Moonlight creeps in one window, then another. JOHN asleep. THE NARRATOR watches the undulating paper. Behind the outside pattern, a faint figure seems to shake it-as if she wanted to get out. THE NARRATOR rises, feels the wall. Paper still. She turns back. JOHN is awake.

next_beat.dialogue: What is it, little girl?

labels.visual_event: He sits up straight-stern, reproachful. She stops.

next_beat.dialogue: My darling, I beg of you, for my sake and for our child's sake, as well as for

labels.visual_event: Harsh daylight. THE NARRATOR on the bed, following the pattern hour after hour-gymnastics of the eye. Bloated curves, debased Romanesque with delirium tremens; slanting waves of optic horror; a horizontal frieze that multiplies the confusion.

next_beat.dialogue: On a pattern like this, by daylight, there is a lack of sequence, a defiance of …

labels.visual_event: Different light. THE NARRATOR watches the first long straight ray from the east window. The paper changes as she watches.

next_beat.dialogue: There is one marked peculiarity about this paper, a thing nobody seems to notice…

labels.visual_event: After meals: enforced lying-down. THE NARRATOR's eyes stay open. She does not sleep. Deceit settles in as habit. JOHN looks in, pleased by her stillness.

next_beat.dialogue: Sleep all you can. It is good for you.

labels.visual_event: When he leaves, she sits up and stares at the wall again.

prev_beat.dialogue: Sleep all you can. It is good for you.

next_beat.dialogue: It is a very bad habit, I am convinced, for, you see, I don't sleep.

labels.visual_event: THE NARRATOR pretends sleep, then rises on innocent excuses. She catches JOHN looking at the wallpaper. Later-JENNIE with her hand on it.

next_beat.dialogue: What are you doing with the paper?

labels.visual_event: THE NARRATOR brighter, secret energy. She eats. JOHN laughs, relieved.

next_beat.dialogue: You seem to be flourishing in spite of your wallpaper.

labels.visual_event: Fog and rain against the barred windows. A yellow smell hangs in the room-gentle at first, then enduring, awful. THE NARRATOR wakes and finds it hanging over her. She sits up, breathing it.

next_beat.dialogue: There is something else about that paper-the smell! It creeps all over the house…

labels.visual_event: THE NARRATOR descends slowly. The yellow smell seems to wait on the treads. She touches the banister, then her hair.

Moonlight. The front pattern moves because the woman behind shakes it. Sometimes many women; sometimes one, crawling fast. In bright spots she keeps still; in shady spots she takes hold of the bars and shakes them hard. She tries to climb through. The pattern strangles-heads through, turned upside-down, eyes white.

next_beat.dialogue: I really have discovered something at last. The front pattern does move-and no w…

labels.visual_event: From the nursery windows, THE NARRATOR's POV: a woman creeping in the long shaded lane; creeping in the dark grape arbors; creeping the long road under the trees-hiding under blackberry vines when a carriage passes.

next_beat.dialogue: I think that woman gets out in the daytime! And I'll tell you why-privately-I've…

labels.visual_event: THE NARRATOR locks the door. Alone, she creeps along the smooch-line on the floor, shoulder fitted to the mark. She rises quickly when a board creaks-John must not suspect.

next_beat.dialogue: I always lock the door when I creep by daylight.

labels.visual_event: Last full night. JOHN stays in town. JENNIE offers to sleep in; THE NARRATOR refuses, sweet and firm.

next_beat.dialogue: I shall undoubtedly rest better for a night all alone.

labels.visual_event: JENNIE goes. Moonlight. The poor thing behind the pattern crawls and shakes. THE NARRATOR leaps up to help-pulls while the figure shakes, shakes while it pulls. Yards of paper peel away in a strip as high as her head, half around the room. Dawn. The awful pattern seems to laugh in the first sun. She sets her jaw.

prev_beat.dialogue: I shall undoubtedly rest better for a night all alone.

next_beat.dialogue: I declared I would finish it to-day!

labels.visual_event: Moving day tomorrow. Furniture already going downstairs. The room empties toward bareness: the great bedstead nailed down, canvas mattress left. JENNIE stares at the stripped wall in amazement.

next_beat.dialogue: I did it out of pure spite at the vicious thing.

labels.visual_event: JENNIE tries to coax her out. THE NARRATOR resists.

prev_beat.dialogue: But I am here, and no person touches this paper but me-not alive!

next_beat.dialogue: It is so quiet and empty and clean now.

labels.visual_event: She fastens the rope-secures herself to the room's logic-and begins to creep, shoulder in the long smooch, round and round on the bare floor.

prev_beat.dialogue: I wonder if they all come out of that wallpaper as I did?

next_beat.dialogue: But I am securely fastened now by my well - hidden rope - you don't get me out i…

labels.visual_event: Pounding at the door. JOHN's voice.

next_beat.dialogue: Open the door! Open the door!

labels.visual_event: THE NARRATOR keeps creeping.

prev_beat.dialogue: Open the door! Open the door!

next_beat.dialogue: John dear! The key is down by the front steps, under a plantain leaf!

labels.visual_event: She repeats it gently, slowly, often. At last his footsteps retreat, return. The key turns. JOHN enters-stops short by the door.

prev_beat.dialogue: The key is down by the front door under a plantain leaf!

next_beat.dialogue: What is the matter? For God's sake, what are you doing!

labels.visual_event: THE NARRATOR keeps creeping. She looks at him over her shoulder.

prev_beat.dialogue: What is the matter? For God's sake, what are you doing!

next_beat.dialogue: I've got out at last, in spite of you and Jane!

labels.visual_event: JOHN faints across her path by the wall. THE NARRATOR pauses only to consider him-then creeps over him, again and again, shoulder to the smooch, circling the room as the yellow light holds.

prev_beat.dialogue: And I've pulled off most of the paper, so you can't put me back!

next_beat.dialogue: Now why should that man have fainted?
